name: Kevin Spacey
role_title: Actor / Producer (American Beauty, House of Cards, The Usual Suspects)
charges_filed: Nine counts of sexual assault and causing a person to engage in penetrative sexual activity without consent, involving four male complainants; alleged incidents spanning 2001–2013, including during his tenure as Artistic Director of the Old Vic theatre in London
jurisdiction: Southwark Crown Court, London, England (UK Crown Prosecution Service)
case_number: CPS authorized charges June 2022; additional charges authorized November 2022 (UK criminal proceeding)
resolution_details: A jury at Southwark Crown Court found Spacey not guilty on all nine remaining counts after a four-week trial. Three additional charges had been dismissed before or during trial. The jury accepted defense arguments including corroborating alibi evidence (work schedules showing Spacey was abroad during one alleged incident period) and found the complainants' testimonies unpersuasive. Spacey testified in his own defense.
source1_description: BBC News – 'Kevin Spacey and accusers settle before civil trial' (March 19, 2026), confirming 2023 criminal acquittal context
source2_description: Wikipedia – 'Kevin Spacey sexual misconduct allegations' — detailed legal timeline
notes: In March 2026, Spacey settled three UK civil (not criminal) lawsuits out of court with his accusers, ending those proceedings. He has not been held liable criminally or civilly in any court. A separate 2022 civil trial in New York (Anthony Rapp lawsuit) also resulted in Spacey being found not liable.
disclaimer: CHARGED ONLY — NOT CONVICTED. Charges were filed but did not result in a conviction.

name: Kevin Spacey
charges_filed: One felony count of indecent assault and battery — alleging Spacey groped an 18-year-old busboy at The Club Car bar in Nantucket, Massachusetts in July 2016
jurisdiction: Nantucket District Court, Cape and Islands District, Massachusetts, USA
case_number: Not publicly disclosed (Cape and Islands District Court, 2019)
resolution_details: Cape and Islands District Attorney Michael O'Keefe dropped the charge, citing 'unavailability of the complaining witness.' The accuser (son of a former Boston TV news anchor) invoked his Fifth Amendment right against self-incrimination during a July 8, 2019 pre-trial hearing concerning a missing cell phone containing potentially exculpatory text messages. Without the accuser's testimony or the phone evidence, prosecutors could not proceed. Spacey had pleaded not guilty. The accuser's separate civil suit had already been dropped on July 5, 2019.
source1_description: NPR – 'Prosecutors Drop Criminal Charges Against Actor Kevin Spacey In Sexual Assault Case' (July 17, 2019)
source2_description: CNN – 'Charge against Kevin Spacey dropped after alleged victim pleads the Fifth' (July 17, 2019)
notes: This was the first criminal case ever filed against Spacey. The Massachusetts case is entirely separate from the 2023 UK criminal acquittal. Spacey had faced up to 5 years in prison if convicted.
disclaimer: CHARGED ONLY — NOT CONVICTED. Charges were filed but were dropped before trial; no conviction resulted.

name: Michael Jackson
charges_filed: Four counts of lewd act upon a child (California Penal Code § 288); four counts of administering an intoxicating agent to a minor for the purpose of committing a felony; one count of attempted child molestation; one count of conspiracy involving child abduction, false imprisonment, and extortion. All counts related to alleged abuse of 13-year-old cancer survivor Gavin Arvizo at Neverland Ranch.
jurisdiction: Santa Barbara County Superior Court, Santa Maria, California, USA (Judge Rodney Melville)
case_number: 1133603 — People of the State of California v. Michael Joseph Jackson
resolution_details: After a 14-week trial featuring 140 witnesses and approximately 32 hours of jury deliberation over seven days, the jury returned not-guilty verdicts on all 14 counts (10 substantive charges, with the conspiracy count encompassing multiple sub-charges). Key factors cited by jurors: (1) the prosecution's timeline was implausible — the alleged molestations supposedly began after the February 2003 Bashir documentary, when Jackson was under intense global scrutiny; (2) the accuser's mother Janet Arvizo was deemed a deeply unreliable witness by jurors (she had committed welfare fraud and admitted prior perjury); (3) no physical evidence of abuse; (4) defense witnesses including Macaulay Culkin and Chris Tucker testified Jackson had never behaved inappropriately with them. The jury foreman stated there 'wasn't a shred of evidence' sufficient for conviction.
source1_description: Santa Barbara County Superior Court — Official Case No. 1133603 (public court documents via caplaw.com)
source2_description: Los Angeles Times – 'Jackson Acquitted on All 10 Counts' (June 14, 2005)
notes: Jackson had previously been investigated in 1993 for alleged abuse of Jordan Chandler. Two grand juries declined to indict. Jackson settled the 1993 civil lawsuit for approximately $23 million without admission of guilt. Only the 2003-2005 case resulted in formal criminal charges. Jackson died June 25, 2009 from acute propofol intoxication.
disclaimer: CHARGED ONLY — NOT CONVICTED. Charges were filed but did not result in a conviction.

name: Robert Blake
role_title: Actor (Baretta TV series, In Cold Blood film)
charges_filed: One count of first-degree murder with special circumstances (death-penalty eligible); two counts of solicitation of murder; one count of conspiracy to commit murder — in the May 4, 2001 shooting death of his wife Bonny Lee Bakley in Studio City, California
jurisdiction: Los Angeles County Superior Court, California, USA
case_number: People v. Robert Blake (Los Angeles County Superior Court; trial began December 20, 2004)
resolution_details: The jury acquitted Blake of first-degree murder and one count of solicitation of murder after a three-month trial. The jury deadlocked 11-1 in favor of acquittal on the second solicitation count; Judge David S. Wesley declared a mistrial on that count and the D.A. declined to retry it. The conspiracy charges had been dismissed pretrial on October 31, 2003. Defense attorney M. Gerald Schwartzbach successfully challenged the credibility of two stuntmen (Ronald Hambleton and Gary McLarty) who claimed Blake solicited them to kill Bakley, noting their histories of drug addiction and criminal conduct. No gunshot residue was conclusively linked to Blake. The jury foreman stated prosecutors 'couldn't put the gun in his hand.' Blake was later found civilly liable for Bakley's wrongful death ($30 million, later reduced to $15 million on appeal). Blake died March 9, 2023.
source1_description: LAPD Newsroom — 'Robert Blake Arrested For Murder Of Bonny Lee Bakley' (April 18, 2002) — official law enforcement record
source2_description: CNN — 'Blake found not guilty in wife's killing' (March 16, 2005)
notes: The murder of Bonny Lee Bakley officially remains unsolved. Blake filed for bankruptcy in 2006 following the civil judgment. His case is frequently compared to the O.J. Simpson case as an example of different outcomes in criminal vs. civil proceedings.
disclaimer: CHARGED ONLY — NOT CONVICTED. Charges were filed but did not result in a conviction.

name: Alec Baldwin
role_title: Actor / Producer (30 Rock, It's Complicated, The Hunt for Red October)
charges_filed: One count of involuntary manslaughter — in connection with the October 21, 2021 fatal shooting of cinematographer Halyna Hutchins (age 42) during a rehearsal for the Western film 'Rust' at Bonanza Creek Ranch near Santa Fe, New Mexico. Baldwin discharged a Colt .45 revolver that had been handed to him as a prop, killing Hutchins and wounding director Joel Souza.
jurisdiction: First Judicial District Court, Santa Fe County, New Mexico, USA (Judge Mary Marlowe Sommer)
case_number: D-101-CR-2024-00037 (second indictment, January 19, 2024); first indictment filed January 31, 2023
resolution_details: Judge Mary Marlowe Sommer dismissed the case WITH PREJUDICE on the third day of trial, citing a serious Brady disclosure violation. Prosecutors had withheld from the defense a supplemental report about a box of ammunition — bullets potentially related to the Rust investigation — delivered to the Santa Fe County Sheriff's Office and filed under a different case number. The judge found authorities 'intentionally and deliberately withheld' the evidence, a finding 'approaching bad faith.' Dismissal with prejudice permanently bars refiling. Background: Baldwin's first indictment (January 2023) was dropped by prosecutors on April 20, 2023, after the defense showed he was being charged under a law not in effect at the time of the shooting. A grand jury re-indicted him January 19, 2024. After the dismissal, the prosecutor filed several appeals that were all denied or withdrawn. The final appeal was withdrawn December 23, 2024, officially closing all criminal proceedings.
source1_description: NPR — 'Judge dismisses actor Alec Baldwin's involuntary manslaughter trial' (July 12, 2024)
source2_description: AP News — 'Prosecutors withdraw appeal of dismissed case against Alec Baldwin' (December 23, 2024)
notes: Armorer Hannah Gutierrez-Reed was separately convicted of involuntary manslaughter (March 6, 2024) and sentenced to 18 months (released May 23, 2025). First AD David Halls pleaded guilty to negligent use of a deadly weapon. Baldwin filed a civil rights lawsuit against the D.A. and other officials in January 2025 alleging wrongful prosecution.
disclaimer: CHARGED ONLY — NOT CONVICTED. Charges were filed but dismissed with prejudice due to prosecutorial misconduct (Brady violation); no conviction resulted.

name: Jussie Smollett
charges_filed: FIRST CHARGING: 16 counts of felony disorderly conduct (false police report) filed February 2019, alleging Smollett staged a racist and homophobic hate attack on himself in Chicago's Streeterville neighborhood on January 29, 2019, and filed false police reports. SECOND CHARGING (Special Prosecutor): 6 counts of felony disorderly conduct — grand jury indictment by Special Prosecutor Dan Webb, 2020.
jurisdiction: Cook County Circuit Court, Illinois, USA
case_number: 19 CR 3104 (2019 original charges); Special Prosecutor re-indictment 2020
resolution_details: The Illinois Supreme Court unanimously (5-0) reversed Smollett's 2021 conviction and ordered the case dismissed on November 21, 2024. The court held that re-prosecuting Smollett after he had fulfilled a binding non-prosecution agreement with the Cook County State's Attorney's Office violated his Fifth Amendment due process rights. Background: In March 2019, State's Attorney Kim Foxx's office dropped the original 16 charges via nolle prosequi in exchange for Smollett forfeiting $10,000 bond and performing 16 hours of community service. This constituted a binding non-prosecution agreement. Special Prosecutor Dan Webb re-indicted Smollett in 2020; a jury convicted him of 5 of 6 felony disorderly conduct counts in December 2021; he was sentenced to 150 days jail (served 6 days) before his release pending appeal. The Illinois Supreme Court held that because Smollett had fully performed his obligations under the 2019 agreement, re-prosecuting him was 'arbitrary, unreasonable, fundamentally unfair, and a violation of due process rights.' The court ordered the case remanded to enter a formal dismissal. Special Prosecutor Webb noted the ruling 'does not clear Jussie Smollett's name — he is not innocent,' but the legal effect is that all criminal charges are dismissed and Smollett has no criminal conviction. He settled a civil lawsuit from the City of Chicago in May 2025 via charitable donation.
source1_description: Winston & Strawn / Special Prosecutor Dan Webb — official statement on Illinois Supreme Court reversal (November 21, 2024)
source2_description: BBC News — 'Illinois Supreme Court overturns Jussie Smollett conviction for hate crime hoax' (November 21, 2024)
notes: Highly unusual resolution: the conviction was overturned not on the merits of the evidence (the IL Supreme Court explicitly acknowledged the evidence of guilt was overwhelming and the jury's verdict was supported by the evidence), but on a constitutional procedural ground (the State's 2019 non-prosecution agreement barred re-prosecution). Both charging episodes involved formal criminal charges. Final legal status: all criminal charges dismissed; no criminal conviction of record.
disclaimer: CHARGED ONLY — NOT CONVICTED. Original charges dropped via nolle prosequi (2019); second prosecution conviction reversed by Illinois Supreme Court on due process grounds (2024) and case ordered dismissed. NOTE: The special prosecutor and the court record both acknowledge the evidence of guilt was substantial; the dismissal rests on procedural grounds only.

name: Snoop Dogg
role_title: Rap Artist / Actor / Media Personality (real name: Calvin Cordozar Broadus Jr.)
charges_filed: First-degree murder, second-degree murder, conspiracy to commit assault, and accessory to murder after the fact — in connection with the August 25, 1993 shooting death of Philip Woldemariam at Woodbine Park in Palms, West Los Angeles. Broadus's bodyguard McKinley 'Malik' Lee fired the fatal shot from Broadus's Jeep Cherokee during a confrontation between rival Crip gang factions.
jurisdiction: Los Angeles County Superior Court, California, USA (Judge Paul G. Flynn)
case_number: People v. Broadus and Lee (Los Angeles County Superior Court; trial November 27, 1995 – February 21, 1996)
resolution_details: After a trial beginning November 27, 1995, the jury (7 men, 5 women) acquitted both Broadus and Lee of first-degree murder, second-degree murder, and conspiracy to commit assault on February 20, 1996. Broadus was also acquitted of the accessory after the fact charge on February 21. The jury deadlocked 9-3 in favor of acquittal on the voluntary manslaughter charge; Judge Flynn declared a mistrial on that count. The D.A. declined to retry either defendant. Key factors: (1) Defense: self-defense — Woldemariam had reached for a weapon when Lee fired; (2) Two prosecution witnesses (Woldemariam's friends) admitted under cross-examination that they had concealed his gun after the shooting in an effort to frame Broadus and Lee; (3) The LAPD had destroyed key evidence (Woldemariam's clothing, bullets, shell casings) while in police custody, severely undermining the prosecution's case. Charges against co-defendant Sean Abrams were dropped before trial.
source1_description: Los Angeles Times — 'Rapper Snoop Doggy Dogg Is Acquitted of Murder' (February 21, 1996)
source2_description: Encyclopedia.com — 'Snoop Doggy Dogg Trial: 1995-96' (comprehensive trial summary including judge, prosecutors, defense lawyers)
notes: Broadus surrendered to police on September 2, 1993 (the evening of the MTV VMAs) and was released on $1 million bail. The wrongful death civil suit filed by Woldemariam's family was settled confidentially in August 1996. Snoop Dogg released the short film 'Murder Was the Case' (1994) semi-autobiographically depicting the events.
disclaimer: CHARGED ONLY — NOT CONVICTED. Charges were filed but did not result in a conviction.

name: John Landis
role_title: Film Director / Producer (Animal House, The Blues Brothers, Trading Places, An American Werewolf in London)
charges_filed: Five counts of involuntary manslaughter — in the July 23, 1982 deaths of actor Vic Morrow (age 53) and child actors Myca Dinh Lee (age 7) and Renee Chen (age 6) on the set of 'Twilight Zone: The Movie' at Indian Dunes Park near Saugus, California. A military helicopter was struck by special-effects explosives and crashed on the actors during a late-night Vietnam War sequence; Morrow and Myca Dinh Lee were decapitated by the rotor blade, and Renee Chen was crushed by the aircraft.
jurisdiction: Los Angeles County Superior Court, California, USA (Judge Roger W. Boren)
case_number: Grand jury indictment June 15, 1983; trial began 1986 (Los Angeles County Superior Court)
resolution_details: After a 10-month trial (the longest criminal trial in California history at that time), with 93 days of prosecution testimony and 71 witnesses, followed by 13 days of closing arguments, the jury of 7 men and 5 women deliberated 9 days and acquitted Landis and all four co-defendants (associate producer George Folsey Jr., unit production manager Dan Allingham, helicopter pilot Dorcey Wingo, and special effects coordinator Paul Stewart) on all 15 counts. Jury foreman Lois Rogers stated: 'We believe this was all an unforeseeable accident — you don't prosecute people for unforeseeable accidents.' The defense successfully pinned proximate cause on special-effects technician James Camomile (who had immunity) for detonating explosives without confirming the helicopter's position. Landis, Folsey, and Allingham had admitted to illegally hiring the children without the required state work permits, but the D.A. did not charge them for that violation. Landis remains the only Hollywood director ever criminally charged for on-set deaths.
source1_description: Los Angeles Times — 'John Landis Not Guilty in 3 Twilight Zone Deaths' (May 29, 1987)
source2_description: Los Angeles Times — 'All Twilight Zone Figures Acquitted: Jurors Clear Director Landis and 4 Others of Manslaughter Charges' (May 30, 1987)
notes: The case directly led to major legislative changes: California tightened child labor laws on film sets and enacted stricter on-set safety regulations. The Directors Guild of America overhauled its safety protocols. The tragedy is considered a turning point in Hollywood production safety. Landis continued to direct major studio films after acquittal. Civil settlements were paid to the families of all three victims.
disclaimer: CHARGED ONLY — NOT CONVICTED. Charges were filed but did not result in a conviction.

name: Errol Flynn
role_title: Film Actor (The Adventures of Robin Hood, Captain Blood, The Sea Hawk) — Warner Bros. contract star
charges_filed: Three counts of statutory rape: two counts involving Peggy Satterlee (alleged sexual intercourse aboard Flynn's yacht 'Sirocco' in August 1941; Satterlee was 15-17 years old) and one count involving Betty Hansen (alleged at a Hollywood party in September 1941; Hansen was 17)
jurisdiction: Los Angeles County Superior Court (Judge Leslie E. Still), California, USA
case_number: Arraignment November 23, 1942; Trial January 11 – February 6, 1943 (Los Angeles County)
resolution_details: After a 21-day trial, a jury (3 men, 9 women — defense attorney Jerry Giesler deliberately selected a majority-female jury) deliberated 24 hours and returned not-guilty verdicts on all three counts. Giesler (retained by Warner Bros.) successfully challenged the credibility of both accusers: he introduced evidence that Satterlee's account was inconsistent with ship logs and that she had lied about her age, and challenged Hansen's account through impeaching witnesses. Flynn testified in his own defense and denied all charges. The case had originally gone to a grand jury, which dismissed the Hansen complaint for insufficient evidence; D.A. Thomas Cochran combined both cases and proceeded to trial. The acquittal paradoxically boosted Flynn's celebrity status and is cited as the origin of the phrase 'in like Flynn.'
source1_description: EBSCO Research Starters — 'Film Star Errol Flynn Is Acquitted of Rape' (authoritative historical summary)
source2_description: Encyclopedia.com — 'Errol Flynn Trial: 1943' (detailed legal and factual analysis)
notes: This is one of the earliest landmark celebrity criminal trials in Hollywood history. Flynn's 1959 memoir 'My Wicked, Wicked Ways' discusses the trial. The D.A. was criticized for overzealous prosecution. Flynn continued his acting career but his health declined; he died October 14, 1959.
disclaimer: CHARGED ONLY — NOT CONVICTED. Charges were filed but did not result in a conviction.

name: Roscoe 'Fatty' Arbuckle
role_title: Silent Film Actor / Comedian — Paramount Pictures star (highest-paid film actor in the world at the time of his arrest, earning $1 million/year)
charges_filed: First-degree murder (later reduced to manslaughter of the first degree) — in the death of actress Virginia Rappe (age 26), who died September 9, 1921 of peritonitis caused by a ruptured bladder, four days after attending Arbuckle's Labor Day party in Suite 1219-1221 of the St. Francis Hotel, San Francisco. Arbuckle was accused by Rappe's companion Maude Delmont of sexually assaulting Rappe, causing her injury.
jurisdiction: San Francisco County Superior Court, California, USA
case_number: Grand jury indictment September 13, 1921 on murder (later reduced to manslaughter). Three trials: Trial 1: November 14 – December 4, 1921; Trial 2: January 11 – February 3, 1922; Trial 3: March 13 – April 12, 1922
resolution_details: After two mistrials (Trial 1: hung jury 10-2 for acquittal; Trial 2: hung jury 10-2 for conviction after defense strategically declined to have Arbuckle testify), the third trial resulted in a unanimous not-guilty verdict after approximately 6 minutes of deliberation. The jury issued an extraordinary formal written apology: 'Acquittal is not enough for Roscoe Arbuckle. We feel that a great injustice has been done him... there was not the slightest proof adduced to connect him in any way with the commission of a crime.' Key factors: Chief accuser Maude Delmont never testified at any trial because prosecutors feared her constantly changing story would collapse; she later admitted to extortion schemes against Arbuckle. Prosecution witnesses admitted under cross-examination to being coerced by D.A. Matthew Brady. Medical evidence showed Rappe had chronic severe cystitis and other pre-existing conditions consistent with natural bladder rupture. The hospital examination found no evidence of rape. Despite acquittal, Arbuckle was blacklisted by the MPPDA (at D.A. Brady's political instigation) and his films were banned. He never fully recovered his career. He died June 29, 1933.
source1_description: The New York Times — 'Arbuckle Acquitted in One-Minute Verdict' (April 13, 1922) — contemporaneous primary source
source2_description: EBSCO Research Starters — 'Film Star Fatty Arbuckle Is Acquitted of Manslaughter' (historical analysis with legal detail)
notes: The Arbuckle scandal (1921-1922) was the first major Hollywood celebrity criminal trial and directly caused: (1) the creation of the Hollywood Production Code (Hays Code); (2) the institution of 'morality clauses' in studio contracts; (3) fundamental changes in how celebrity private lives were reported by the press. D.A. Matthew Brady, who sought the death penalty and reportedly had gubernatorial ambitions, was widely criticized for prosecutorial misconduct. The case is a landmark in legal history for demonstrating the dangers of media-driven prosecution.
disclaimer: CHARGED ONLY — NOT CONVICTED. Charges were filed but did not result in a conviction. The jury issued a formal written apology upon acquittal.

name: Sean 'Puffy' Combs (Puff Daddy / P. Diddy)
role_title: Music Producer / Rapper / Entrepreneur — Founder, Bad Boy Records
charges_filed: Five counts: criminal possession of a weapon in the second degree; criminal possession of a weapon in the third degree; bribery (second degree) — alleging Combs offered his chauffeur Wardel Fenderson $50,000 and a diamond ring to claim ownership of a 9mm pistol found in Combs's vehicle; and related charges. Stemming from the December 27, 1999 shooting at Club New York on West 43rd Street, Manhattan, in which three patrons were wounded.
jurisdiction: New York State Supreme Court, New York County (Manhattan), New York, USA
case_number: New York State Supreme Court, New York County (2001 trial; trial began February 2001)
resolution_details: After a 7-week trial with more than 60 witnesses, the jury deliberated 22 hours and returned not-guilty verdicts on all five counts. Combs testified in his own defense, claiming he fired in self-defense believing he was being shot at. Defense attorneys Benjamin Brafman and Johnnie Cochran successfully argued the prosecution had relied on witnesses who were financially interested in the outcome (Fenderson and two wounded victims all had pending civil suits against Combs) and that there was insufficient credible evidence tying Combs to the weapon or the shooting. Combs's bodyguard Anthony 'Wolf' Jones was also acquitted. Combs protégé Shyne (Jamal Barrow) was convicted of assault, reckless endangerment, and weapon possession; he served approximately 9 years.
source2_description: Los Angeles Times — ''Puffy' Combs Not Guilty in Gun Case' (March 17, 2001)
notes: CRITICAL NOTE: This entry covers ONLY the 2001 acquittal in the Club New York nightclub shooting case. Sean Combs was subsequently indicted on federal sex trafficking and racketeering charges in September 2024 (SDNY, Case No. 24-cr-542). In July 2025, a federal jury CONVICTED him on two counts of transportation for prostitution (Mann Act violations) and acquitted him of racketeering conspiracy and sex trafficking. He was sentenced to 50 months in federal prison (October 3, 2025) and is appealing that conviction as of March 2026. He is therefore a CONVICTED PERSON with respect to the 2025 federal case and should not be classified as 'not convicted' in that context.
disclaimer: CHARGED ONLY — NOT CONVICTED (2001 nightclub shooting case only). SEPARATE CASE: Combs was CONVICTED in July 2025 on two federal prostitution-related charges and sentenced to federal prison in October 2025. This entry covers only the 2001 acquittal.

name: Nate Parker
role_title: Actor / Director / Writer (The Birth of a Nation, 2016; college athlete — Penn State wrestler at time of charges)
charges_filed: Involuntary deviate sexual intercourse, sexual assault, and indecent assault — involving a female Penn State University student (age 18) who alleged she was unconscious and non-consenting when Parker and his wrestling roommate/co-defendant Jean Celestin sexually assaulted her in their off-campus apartment in 1999
jurisdiction: Centre County Court of Common Pleas, Bellefonte, Pennsylvania, USA
case_number: Commonwealth v. Nate Parker (Centre County, PA, criminal case 1999-2001)
resolution_details: After a three-day trial, the jury acquitted Parker of all criminal charges. The defense emphasized a prior consensual sexual relationship between Parker and the accuser and challenged the prosecution's evidence that she was unconscious and non-consenting during the incident. Co-defendant Jean Celestin was convicted of sexual assault and sentenced to 6 months in prison; however, his conviction was overturned on appeal because the victim declined to testify at the re-trial, and the case against Celestin was not retried. Parker transferred to Oklahoma State University. The accuser received a $17,500 civil settlement from Penn State. She died by suicide in 2012. The case resurfaced prominently in 2016 when Parker's Sundance film 'The Birth of a Nation' debuted, severely impacting its awards campaign and commercial performance.
source1_description: The New Yorker — 'The Public Trial of Nate Parker' (September 2, 2016)
source2_description: TIME — 'Nate Parker a Rape Trial and Release of Birth of a Nation' (August 17, 2016)
notes: Parker has maintained his innocence and stated that the relationship was consensual. The case's resurfacing in 2016 led to widespread public debate about how Hollywood should handle historical criminal charges against filmmakers when evaluating their work.
disclaimer: CHARGED ONLY — NOT CONVICTED. Charges were filed but did not result in a conviction.

name: Cuba Gooding Jr.
role_title: Actor (Jerry Maguire — Academy Award Best Supporting Actor 1997; Boyz n the Hood; Radio)
charges_filed: Six misdemeanor counts of forcible touching under New York Penal Law § 130.52 — involving three women who alleged Gooding engaged in unwanted groping and touching at New York City nightclubs between 2018 and 2019
jurisdiction: New York County Criminal Court (Manhattan), New York, USA
case_number: People v. Cuba Gooding Jr. (Manhattan Criminal Court, Docket No. 2019NY032877)
resolution_details: After protracted proceedings in which more than 30 additional women came forward with similar allegations, Gooding entered a conditional guilty plea in April 2022 to one misdemeanor count of forcible touching relating to one accuser (cocktail waitress Natasha Ashby). He completed six months of mandatory alcohol and behavioral counseling. On October 13, 2022, Gooding was permitted to withdraw his misdemeanor guilty plea and instead plead guilty to a non-criminal harassment violation under New York law — meaning all six original criminal misdemeanor counts were fully resolved without a criminal conviction on his record. The criminal charges against the other two accusers were dismissed as part of the plea agreement. District Attorney Alvin Bragg's office faced criticism from victim advocates who called the resolution 'a prosecutorial gift to a celebrity.' Gooding separately settled a federal civil lawsuit in June 2023 (filed by a woman alleging rape in 2013) moments before jury selection was to begin; terms undisclosed.
source1_description: The Independent — 'Actor Cuba Gooding Jr resolved his New York City forcible touching case with a guilty plea to a lesser charge and no jail time' (October 13, 2022)
source2_description: NPR — 'Cuba Gooding Jr. settles a civil sex abuse case just as trial was set to begin' (June 6, 2023)
notes: The resolution is nuanced: Gooding was formally charged with six misdemeanor criminal counts, entered a conditional guilty plea to one, fulfilled the conditions, then withdrew the plea and substituted a non-criminal harassment violation — leaving him with no criminal record. A New York harassment 'violation' is not a 'crime' under state law. Victim advocates characterized the outcome as insufficient accountability.
disclaimer: CHARGED ONLY — NOT CONVICTED. Six criminal misdemeanor charges were filed. Final resolution was a plea to a non-criminal harassment violation; no criminal conviction on record.

name: O.J. Simpson
role_title: NFL Running Back / Actor / Broadcaster (The Naked Gun film series; NFL Hall of Fame; Hertz spokesman)
charges_filed: Two counts of first-degree murder with special circumstances — making him eligible for the death penalty — in the June 12, 1994 stabbing deaths of his ex-wife Nicole Brown Simpson (age 35) and her friend Ronald Goldman (age 25) outside Nicole's Brentwood, Los Angeles home
jurisdiction: Los Angeles County Superior Court, California, USA (Judge Lance Ito)
case_number: People of the State of California v. Orenthal James Simpson, Case No. BA097211
resolution_details: After a 134-day trial (the longest in California history at the time), featuring approximately 150 witnesses, 857 pieces of evidence, and nine months of proceedings, the jury of 9 Black, 2 White, and 1 Hispanic members deliberated approximately 4 hours over two days and returned not-guilty verdicts on both murder counts. The verdict was watched live by an estimated 150 million viewers. The defense 'Dream Team' (Johnnie Cochran, Robert Shapiro, F. Lee Bailey, Barry Scheck, Peter Neufeld) successfully argued: (1) systematic LAPD mishandling and contamination of DNA evidence; (2) evidence planting — a bloody glove found at Simpson's Rockingham estate didn't fit him when demonstrated in court ('If it doesn't fit, you must acquit'); (3) Detective Mark Fuhrman committed perjury by denying use of racial slurs (Fuhrman later pleaded no contest to perjury); (4) a rush to judgment driven by anti-Black bias at LAPD. Simpson had been arrested on June 17, 1994, following the nationally televised 'slow-speed chase' in his white Bronco. In February 1997, a civil jury found Simpson liable for both deaths ($33.5 million judgment, largely uncollected). Simpson was later convicted of unrelated armed robbery and kidnapping in Nevada in 2008. He died April 10, 2024.
source1_description: Los Angeles County Superior Court — Case No. BA097211 (official court record, public)
source2_description: Wikipedia — 'O.J. Simpson murder case' — comprehensive trial record with extensive sourcing
notes: This is the single most-watched criminal trial in American television history and a defining cultural and legal event. Simpson's 1995 acquittal is frequently compared to Robert Blake's 2005 acquittal as examples of celebrity justice. NOTE: Simpson WAS subsequently convicted on unrelated charges (2008 Nevada armed robbery/kidnapping; sentenced to 33 years, paroled 2017). This entry covers only the 1994-1995 murder case acquittal.
disclaimer: CHARGED ONLY — NOT CONVICTED (1994-95 murder case). Murder charges were filed but did not result in a conviction. NOTE: Simpson was later convicted in a separate 2008 case.

name: Woody Harrelson
role_title: Actor / Activist (Cheers TV series, Natural Born Killers, No Country for Old Men, True Detective)
charges_filed: Misdemeanor criminal charge of cultivating marijuana — Harrelson deliberately planted four industrial hemp seeds in Lee County, Kentucky in June 1996 as a civil disobedience protest to challenge the constitutionality of Kentucky's hemp prohibition law (which made no distinction between industrial hemp and high-THC marijuana)
jurisdiction: Lee County Circuit Court, Kentucky, USA (on appeal from Lee County District Court)
case_number: Commonwealth v. Harrelson (Lee County, Kentucky District/Circuit Court, 1996-2000)
resolution_details: The district court initially found Harrelson guilty. He appealed to the Lee County Circuit Court, where a jury found him not guilty. The defense successfully argued that industrial hemp (Cannabis sativa with negligible THC content) was botanically and legally distinct from marijuana, and that Harrelson's deliberate act of planting seeds was a constitutionally protected act of civil disobedience to test an unjust law. Harrelson had publicly announced his intention to plant the seeds before doing so. The acquittal was significant for hemp advocacy groups.
source1_description: Wikipedia — 'Woody Harrelson' (documents the hemp trial and acquittal)
source2_description: Comic Basics — 'Hollywood Actors Who Were Acquitted in Court' (March 2026) — includes Harrelson acquittal
notes: Harrelson is a well-known hemp and cannabis legalization advocate. The case helped build momentum for the eventual federal legalization of industrial hemp under the 2018 Agriculture Improvement Act (Farm Bill). The seeds he planted had negligible THC content consistent with industrial hemp.
disclaimer: CHARGED ONLY — NOT CONVICTED. Criminal charge was filed but did not result in a conviction upon appeal.

name: Will Smith
role_title: Rapper / Actor (The Fresh Prince of Bel-Air; early career: Fresh Prince rap duo with DJ Jazzy Jeff)
charges_filed: Aggravated assault, criminal conspiracy, simple assault, and recklessly endangering another person — charges arising from a 1989 confrontation at a Philadelphia recording studio in which Smith's bodyguard/associate Charles 'Charlie Mack' Alston allegedly beat William Hendricks so severely that Hendricks nearly lost an eye and required six stitches. Smith was charged as a co-conspirator for allegedly directing the attack.
case_number: Philadelphia criminal court (1989; records not digitized)
resolution_details: The criminal charges against Smith — including the most serious aggravated assault charge — were dismissed after complainant William Hendricks declined to cooperate with prosecutors. Smith described the incident in his 2021 memoir 'Will,' acknowledging Charlie Mack beat Hendricks. Reports indicate Hendricks later received a $25,000 payment from Smith. The charges were dismissed and Smith was not convicted of any offense arising from this incident. The incident occurred weeks after Smith and DJ Jazzy Jeff won the first-ever Grammy Award for Best Rap Performance.
source1_description: Instagram — contemporaneous account of 1989 charges and dismissal (public social media record documenting the event)
source2_description: Reddit/r/entertainment — 'Will Smith's Mugshot From Brutal 1989 Assault Resurfaces Following Oscars Slap' (April 2022) — documents the charges and their dismissal
notes: Court records from this period are not digitized. The charges and their dismissal are documented through contemporaneous news accounts and Smith's own memoir. Charlie Mack Alston (the person who actually committed the physical assault) was reportedly not charged. The incident received renewed media attention following Smith's March 2022 Oscars slap of Chris Rock.
disclaimer: CHARGED ONLY — NOT CONVICTED. Criminal charges were filed but dismissed; no conviction resulted.

name: Matthew McConaughey
role_title: Actor (Dazed and Confused, Dallas Buyers Club, True Detective, Interstellar)
charges_filed: Possession of marijuana and possession of drug paraphernalia — charged following a 4:00 AM police response to a noise complaint at his Austin, Texas home on October 25, 1999. Police found McConaughey dancing naked while playing bongo drums; a marijuana pipe and drug residue were discovered. He was also charged with resisting transportation.
jurisdiction: Austin Municipal Court, Travis County, Texas, USA
resolution_details: The marijuana possession and drug paraphernalia charges were dropped. McConaughey paid a $50 fine on the noise violation. The resisting transportation charge was also not pursued. He was not convicted of any drug-related offense. The incident generated extensive media coverage under the nickname 'Naked Bongo Man' but did not materially impact his acting career.
source1_description: Wikipedia — 'Matthew McConaughey' (documents 1999 arrest and charges resolution)
source2_description: Comic Basics — 'Hollywood Actors Who Were Acquitted in Court' (2026) — includes McConaughey reference
notes: McConaughey has spoken publicly about the incident in multiple interviews. The arrest occurred during a transitional period in his career before his mid-career 'McConaissance' dramatic resurgence. The drug charges being dropped reflects standard prosecutorial discretion for first-time minor marijuana offenses.
disclaimer: CHARGED ONLY — NOT CONVICTED on drug charges. Charges were filed but dropped; only a noise violation fine was imposed.

name: Vince Vaughn
role_title: Actor / Producer (Wedding Crashers, Swingers, Old School, Anchorman)
charges_filed: Assault with a deadly weapon and multiple related assault charges — following a May 2001 bar fight in Wilmington, North Carolina during production of the film 'Domestic Disturbance.' Vaughn and co-defendant Steve Buscemi were involved in an altercation at a nightclub in which Buscemi was stabbed in the face and neck.
jurisdiction: New Hanover County, North Carolina, USA
case_number: New Hanover County, NC criminal case (2001)
resolution_details: The most serious charges against Vaughn, including assault with a deadly weapon, were dropped. The altercation involved a group who allegedly attacked Vaughn's group; Buscemi (who was stabbed) was the victim in the incident, not a defendant. Vaughn's charges arose from his alleged participation in the physical altercation. No prosecution proceeded. The case was resolved without any criminal conviction against Vaughn.
source1_description: Wikipedia — 'Vince Vaughn' (documents the 2001 Wilmington incident and charges)
source2_description: Comic Basics — 'Hollywood Actors Who Were Acquitted in Court' (2026) — includes Vaughn reference
notes: Steve Buscemi was stabbed multiple times and required hospital treatment. Multiple people were involved in the brawl. The incident occurred during a period when Vaughn was establishing himself as a leading man in Hollywood. His career was not materially impacted.
disclaimer: CHARGED ONLY — NOT CONVICTED. Charges were filed but dropped; no criminal conviction resulted.
